coredata.dc:title: Sandwich assay for β-lactoglobulin in infant food formula based on a hierarchically architectured antifouling capture probe and fluorescent recognition probe
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Huan
Jia, Donghui
Shuai, Jiangbing
Zhang, Xiaofeng
Wang, Shunyu
Wang, Min
Li, Ke
Fu, Linglin
coredata.dc:description: Tracing the presence of allergenic β-lactoglobulin (β-Lg) in infant foods is an urgent need, but the interference from the protein-rich matrix often hampered the detection accuracy. Here, we developed a sandwich assay for β-Lg in infant food formula based on a hierarchically architectured antifouling capture probe and fluorescent recognition probe. The antifouling capture probe was constructed from the polydopamine-coated magnetic particles (Fe3O4@PDA), which was modified with repeated glutamic acid-lysine (EK) antifouling peptide and aptamer towards β-Lg. The spatial arrangement of these ligands on the Fe3O4@PDA surface was carefully tailored. Furthermore, a fluorescent recognition probe based on aptamer-modified silica-doped carbon quantum dot was developed to explore a sandwich assay for β-Lg with the capture probe. The sandwich assay was proved to have high potential in detecting β-Lg in commercially available infant food samples. The work provided a new approach to developing detection methods with matrix interference-resistant properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Food allergy
Infant food formula
Nonspecific protein adsorption
Aptamer
Sandwich assay

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of antifouling property of the planar surface
Antifouling property of the Fe3O4@PDA@EK particles
Construction of the capture probe based on Fe3O4@PDA
Construction of the fluorescent recognition probe
Detection of β-Lg by the sandwich assay
Analysis of the commercial infant food formula samples
Antifouling property of the EK peptide-modified interface
Spatial arrangement of the anti-fouling and recognition ligands
Construction of the fluorescent recognition probe
Method validation of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Antifouling property of the EK peptides. (A) SPR spectra tested in milk whole protein and soy whole protein. (B) WCA images of the bare chip and EK peptide-modified chips. (C) Antifouling ability of the EK peptide modified Fe3O4@PDA. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. n = 3.

Effects of the spatial arrangement and feeding ratio of EK10 and APT1 on antifouling property and active recognition functionality. (A) Schematic diagram of two spatial arrangements based on EK10 and APT1 (not to scale). (B) Protein adsorption of the Fe3O4@PDA with different EK10 and APT1 spatial arrangements. (C) Optimization of the feed ratio of EK10 and APT1. Group A (500 pmol APT1), Group B (1.5 nmol EK10), Group C (100 pmol APT1 + 1.2 nmol EK10), Group D (200 pmol APT1 + 900 pmol EK10), and Group E (300 pmol APT1 + 600 pmol EK10). *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001. n = 3.

Construction and characterization of the capture probe and fluorescent recognition probe. (A) Schematic diagram of the detection principle. (B) Hydrodynamic size of the capture probe. (C) Zeta potential of the capture probe. (D) UV–Vis spectra of the Si-CD and Si-CD-APT2. (E) Hydrodynamic size of the Si-CD and Si-CD-APT2. (F) Zeta potential of the Si-CD and Si-CD-APT2. (G) Fluorescence spectra of the Si-CD and Si-CD-APT2.

Optimization of the detection conditions of the sandwich assay. (a) Optimization of the buffer solution. (b) Optimization of the additive amounts of fluorescent recognition probes. (c) Optimization of the incubation temperature. n = 3.

Detection performance of the sandwich assay. (A) The standard curve of the sandwich assay. (B) Specificity of the sandwich assay. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 in comparison to the β-Lg group. n = 3.
β-Lg contents in commercial samples detected by the sandwich assay and ELISA.

head.ce:title: Sandwich assay for β-lactoglobulin in infant food formula based on a hierarchically architectured antifouling capture probe and fluorescent recognition probe

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: TEDA Institute of Biological Sciences and Biotechnology
Key Laboratory of Molecular Microbiology and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: TEDA Institute of Biological Sciences and Biotechnology, Key Laboratory of Molecular Microbiology and Technology, Ministry of Education, Nankai University, Tianjin 300457, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tracing the presence of allergenic β-lactoglobulin (β-Lg) in infant foods is an urgent need, but the interference from the protein-rich matrix often hampered the detection accuracy. Here, we developed a sandwich assay for β-Lg in infant food formula based on a hierarchically architectured antifouling capture probe and fluorescent recognition probe. The antifouling capture probe was constructed from the polydopamine-coated magnetic particles (Fe3O4@PDA), which was modified with repeated glutamic acid-lysine (EK) antifouling peptide and aptamer towards β-Lg. The spatial arrangement of these ligands on the Fe3O4@PDA surface was carefully tailored. Furthermore, a fluorescent recognition probe based on aptamer-modified silica-doped carbon quantum dot was developed to explore a sandwich assay for β-Lg with the capture probe. The sandwich assay was proved to have high potential in detecting β-Lg in commercially available infant food samples. The work provided a new approach to developing detection methods with matrix interference-resistant properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The incidence of food allergy has gradually increased worldwide over the past years, which has been called the “second wave” allergy epidemic after asthma (Prescott & Allen, 2011). It has been reported that 8 % of children are allergic to food, with 1/3 of them being allergic to cow milk (Sicherer & Sampson, 2018). Since the immune system of infants and young children is not well developed, and most of their protein intake after breastfeeding comes from cow milk protein, sensitised infants and young children inevitably suffer from the threat of cow milk allergy. Therefore, infant food formulas depleted of cow’s milk protein (e.g. soy infant formula powder), or containing hydrolyzed cow milk protein (e.g. moderate/deep hydrolyzed milk protein powder) have been developed over the years (Inuo et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2020). To avoid the occurrence of allergies, determination of the presence of cow milk allergen is of extreme importance. Studies have shown that about 82 % of patients with cow milk allergy are allergic to β-lactoglobulin (β-Lg) (Jia, Zhu, Wang, Peng, & Shi, 2022; Y. Li et al., 2022). Therefore, β-Lg can be used as a biomarker in the detection of allergenic potential in the alternative infant food formula on the market.

At present, the detection of β-Lg mainly relies on methods such as enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) (He, Li, Gao, Tong, & Chen, 2017), immunochromatography (Galan-Malo et al., 2019), protein microarray technology (Badran, Morais, & Maquieira, 2017), and so on. However, false results are often observed due to the alterations induced by the food processing methods to different proteins (e.g. β-Lg) (Villa, Moura, Costa, & Mafra, 2022) and/or by the interaction of the detection probes with other food components present in matrix (Dong et al., 2019; Ramkumar et al., 2018). To more accurately trace the presence of β-Lg, it is necessary to establish a detection method that can reduce the interaction and adsorption of non-specific proteins to avoid false results. In the existing research, antifouling materials are often used to reduce the interaction of matrix components with the interface of a detection system. These materials could endow an interface with an electrostatic effect or hydrogen bonding-induced hydration layer, which inhibits the matrix components from accessing or interacting, reducing the undesired interference (Molino et al., 2018). Polyethylene glycol (PEG) has been recognized as the golden standard for antifouling materials (Oberhaus et al., 2021). However, PEG is susceptible to oxidization, which affects the antifouling property (Hamami, Mars, & Raouafi, 2021). In recent years, zwitterionic materials such as phosphorylcholine (PC), polysulfobetaine (PSB), and polycarboxybetaine (PCB) were developed as antifouling materials (Du et al., 2017; B. Li et al., 2019; Ong et al., 2017). For instance, Vaisocherova-Lisalova et al. (Vaisocherova-Lisalova et al., 2016) constructed a PCB-acrylamide (pCBAA) modified surface plasma resonance (SPR) biosensor, which could rapidly and sensitively detect pathogenic bacteria in food samples with little interference from the matrix components. However, the complexity in the synthesis of these zwitterionic polymers limits their further application (Vaisocherova-Lisalova et al., 2016). Jiang’s group has found that zwitterionic peptides composed of repeated glutamic acid (E) and lysine (K) sequences showed great antifouling properties. The excellent nonspecific protein resistance ability of the EK peptides was due to the strong hydration effect via ionic solvation around charged residues (Chen, Cao, & Jiang, 2009). In addition, EK peptides could overcome the shortcomings of PEG, and their physical and chemical properties (hydrophilicity, charge, fixed end, etc.) could be regulated by tailoring the sequence of amino acids (Ederth, Lerm, Orihuela, & Rittschof, 2019; Ham, Park, Kurutz, Szleifer, & Messersmith, 2013). At present, there are little report on the application of EK peptides in eliminating the matrix interference of infant food formula, which features a typical protein-rich characteristic.

Achieving efficient recognition of target analytes is of equal importance as eliminating the interference from matrices. However, there is a dilemma that antifouling surface modification often affects the functionality of recognition components (Baggerman, Smulders, & Zuilhof, 2019; Wiarachai, Vilaivan, Iwasaki, & Hoven, 2016). Therefore, for the detection of allergens in infant food formula, it is a good attempt to design detection methods balancing the nonspecific protein resistance of antifouling interfaces and the specific targeting of recognition elements. In recent years, aptamers have been considered promising recognition elements since they show high affinity and specificity to the target and are easy to synthesize (Huang et al., 2021), making them excellent recognition elements for β-Lg. In the collaborative application of antifouling materials and aptamers in the antifouling electrochemical biosensor, it was realized that their spatial arrangement had a great influence on the detection performance (Liu, Fan, Hou, Gao, & Luo, 2021; G. Wang et al., 2018). Therefore, it is important to balance the relationship between interference resistance and active recognition with the consideration of spatial arrangement when using antifouling materials and aptamers to construct detection probes for β-Lg in infant food formula.

In this work, we developed a sandwich fluorescent assay for β-Lg in a complex food matrix based on a hierarchical architectured antifouling capture probe and fluorescent recognition probe. Firstly, the impact of the sequence length of EK peptides on the antifouling ability of the surface modification methods was studied, and the EK peptide with optimized hydrophilicity and protein resistance was selected for the construction of the capture probe. The antifouling capture probe was developed by modifying the polydopamine-coated magnetic particles (Fe3O4@PDA) with antifouling EK peptide and specific aptamer towards β-Lg (APT1). Then, the spatial arrangement of the EK peptide and APT1 on the capture probe was carefully studied. Furthermore, a fluorescent recognition probe was developed by conjugating another APT towards β-Lg (APT2) on the silica-doped carbon quantum dot (Si-CD), which cooperated with the capture probe to construct a sandwich assay. The quantitative analysis of β-Lg in commercial samples was also carried out. The work provided an approach to tackling the dilemma between interference-resistance and active recognition and paved a new way for the interference-free determination of food allergens in the protein-rich matrix.

The Fe3O4 magnetic nanoparticle was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). Dopamine hydrochloride, acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), trimethylol aminomethane (Tris), hydrochloric acid (HCl). Gold chips for water contact angle (WCA) analysis and atomic force microscopy (AFM) observation were purchased from Boshi Trading Co., Ltd (Shengzhou, China). SPR chips were purchased from Russell Technologies Co., Ltd (Hangzhou, China). The Modified Bradford Protein Assay Kit was purchased from Sangon (Shanghai, China). The bicinchoninic acid (BCA) assay and whole protein extraction kit were purchased from KeyGEN BioTECH Corp., Ltd (Jiangsu, China). Isopropyl alcohol (C3H8O), β-Lg, and N-(3-Dimethylaminopropyl)-N’-ethyl carbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), N-Hydroxy succinimide (NHS) were purchased from Macklin (Shanghai, China). CPPPPEKEK (EK2), CPPPPEKEKEKEKEK (EK5), and CPPPPEKEKEKEKEKEKEKEKEKEK (EK10) peptides with purity of 90 % were purchased from ChinaPeptides Co., LTD (Suzhou, China). Silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CD) were purchased from Xianfeng Nanomaterial Technology Co. LTD (Jiangsu, China). The two APT against β-Lg were adopted from previous work (Eissa & Zourob, 2017; Tah et al., 2018): APT1 (5′-SH-C6-CGACGATCGGACCGCAGTACCCACCCACCAGCCCCAACATCATGCCCATCCGTGTGTG, 5′-COOH-CGACGATCGGACCGCAGTACCCACCCACCAGCCCCAACATCATGCCCATCCGTGTGTG), and APT2 (5′-NH2-C6-ATACCAGCTTATTCAATTCGACGATCGGACCGCAGTACCCACCCACCAGCCCCAACATCATGCCCATCCGTGTGTGAGATAGTAAGTGCAATCT) were synthesized by Sangon (Shanghai, China). Infant formula samples were purchased from online markets (JD.COM and Taobao.com). The water used was all Milli-Q water (Millipore, Massachusetts, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of antifouling property of the planar surface
ce:section.ce:para: Bare gold chips were cleaned by Milli-Q water three times and dried with nitrogen. The gold chips were modified with 0.4 mg/mL EK2, EK5, and EK10 for 24 h. Then the chip was carefully washed with Milli-Q water three times and dried with nitrogen. The static water contact angle of the chips was measured by WCA measurements (Dataphysics, OCA50, Germany). To evaluate the antifouling property, the chips were treated with 1 mL of 1 mg/mL β-Lg, milk whole protein (milk-based infant formula powder), and soy whole protein (soy-based infant formula powder) for 24 h, respectively. Then the chips were washed three times by Milli-Q water and analyzed by the AFM (Bruker, Dimension Icon, Germany). Similarly, the SPR chips were modified with EK2, EK5, and EK10 peptides respectively, and subjected to the SPR test: phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 2.89 g/L Na2HPO4·12H2O, 0.2 g/L KH2PO4, 8 g/L NaCl, 0.2 g/L KCl, pH 7.4) was first run at a flow rate of 200 μL/min for 15 min to obtain a stable baseline; then 1 mg/mL protein solution was fed at a flow rate of 10 μL/min for 10 min; chips were washed by PBS until the baseline was stabilized. The response value was collected by SPR spectroscopy (Nicoya, OpenSPR, Canada).

The Fe3O4@PDA particles were prepared similarly as the previously reported work (Y. Wang, Li, Li, Peng, & Fu, 2022). Briefly, 20 mg of Fe3O4 particles were mixed with 1 mg/mL dopamine hydrochloride in Tris-HCl (pH 8.5) and oscillated for 3 h. The obtained Fe3O4@PDA was collected by magnetic separation and cleaned with 3 % (v/v) acetic acid containing 0.1 % (w/v) SDS. The Fe3O4@PDA was cleaned until the supernatant showed no characteristic peaks of dopamine in the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra (Shimazu, UV2600, Japan). Dynamic light scattering (Malvern, Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Britain), transmission electron microscope (Tecnai F30, Philips-FEI, Netherlands), and scanning electron microscope (Zeiss Auriga, Helios G3 CX, Germany) were used to characterize the Fe3O4@PDA particles. The Fe3O4@PDA@EK particles were prepared by incubating 1 mL of 0.5 mg/mL Fe3O4@PDA in PBS solution with 10 nmol EK2, EK5, and EK10 for 12 h, respectively. After incubation, the particles were magnetically separated and cleaned by PBS to remove the free EK peptides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling property of the Fe3O4@PDA@EK particles
ce:section.ce:para: The 1 mL of Fe3O4@PDA@EK was incubated with 10 µg of β-Lg, milk whole protein, and soy whole protein, respectively. After incubation, the supernatant was collected through 1 h centrifugation at 16,000 g. The protein content in the supernatant was measured by the Modified Bradford Protein Assay Kit. The amount of protein adsorbed on the particles was obtained by the differential subtraction method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the capture probe based on Fe3O4@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: The mixed modification and hierarchical modification of EK10 and APT were conducted on the Fe3O4@PDA particle. For the mixed modified Fe3O4@PDA particle, 1.3 nmol of EK10 and 100 pmol of thiol-modified APT1 were added to 1 mL of 0.5 mg/mL Fe3O4@PDA solution and incubated overnight under oscillations. Then it was washed 3 times. For the hierarchical modified Fe3O4@PDA particle, 1.3 nmol of EK10 was incubated overnight with 1 mL of 0.5 mg/mL Fe3O4@PDA solution, and then 100 pmol of carboxyl-modified APT1 was added after EDC and NHS activation. After 6 h incubation, the particles were washed 3 times.

The antifouling properties of the two particles were investigated by 10 μg of β-Lg, milk whole protein, and soy whole protein, respectively. Briefly, the probes were incubated with the proteins for 2 h, and centrifuged at 16,000 g for 1 h. The protein content of the supernatant was measured using the Modified Bradford Protein Assay Kit, which was converted to the protein adsorption amount on the particles.

The capture probe was optimized by alternating the feed ratio of the two ligands. Control Group without any modification, Group A with only APT modification (500 pmol APT1), Group B (1.5 nmol EK10), Group C (100 pmol APT1 + 1.2 nmol EK10), Group D (200 pmol APT1 + 900 pmol EK10), and Group E (300 pmol APT1 + 600 pmol EK10) were carefully evaluated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the fluorescent recognition probe
ce:section.ce:para: Five mL of 0.1 mg/mL silicon-doped carbon quantum dots (Si-CD) were added with 11 mg of EDC and 10 mg of NHS at pH 5.0 and oscillated for 30 min. Then the solution pH was adjusted to 7.4 and 1.2 nmol of APT2 was added. It was oscillated for 2 h and ultrafiltrated at 3000 g for 3 times. The purified recognition probe was stored at 4 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of β-Lg by the sandwich assay
ce:section.ce:para: For the β-Lg detection, 1 mL of 0.5 mg/mL capture probe was mixed with β-Lg and incubated at 25 °C for 2 h. Then it was washed by PBS for three times. Next, the fluorescent recognition probe (100 pmoL of APT2) was added and oscillated at 25 °C for 1 h. The mixed solution was then washed by PBS for three times. After resuspension in 1 mL PBS, the fluorescence signal at 520 nm emission (480 nm excitation) was measured.

To explore the specificity of the method, a capture probe with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was added with the nonspecific protein with a final concentration of 2 µg/mL. The mixture was incubated and oscillated for 2 h. Then the fluorescent probe was added and further incubated for 1 h. The mixed solution was then washed by PBS three times. After resuspension in 1 mL PBS, the fluorescence signal at 520 nm emission (480 nm excitation) was measured.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the commercial infant food formula samples
ce:section.ce:para: The whole protein of infant formula powder was extracted by direct dissolution in warm water. The whole protein of infant rice flour was extracted by adding 0.1 g of rice flour into 1 mL of 10 mM acetic acid-sodium acetate buffer (pH 6.0). Then it was oscillated for 1 h and added with 0.5 mL 50 mM sodium carbonate, which was subjected to a 30 °C water bath for 1 h incubation. After 4000 rpm centrifugation for 5 min, the supernatant was collected and subjected to analysis. A whole protein extraction kit was used for infant grain noodles and nutritious teethers. The apple puree and apple juice were centrifuged at 10,000 g for 30 min and the supernatant was collected for subsequent analysis. β-Lg was detected using the method described in Section 2.7. A commercial ELISA kit was used to detect β-Lg for comparison by following the instruction manual.

The statistical significance was analyzed through the one-way ANOVA analysis of variance by GraphPad prism software 8.0.2. All experiments were performed three times. The significant difference in experimental results was represented by “*”, *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antifouling property of the EK peptide-modified interface
ce:section.ce:para: Inhibiting matrix interference through antifouling interface modification has been considered a promising way to improve the accuracy of a detection method (Yandi et al., 2014). Among the various antifouling materials, EK peptide exhibits advantages such as excellent antifouling performance, easy preparation, and feasibility for modification (Walker et al., 2019). Therefore, we chose the EK peptide to construct a capture probe for β-Lg. Firstly, the antifouling ability of EK peptides with different sequence lengths, EK2, EK5, and EK10, was studied. The EK peptide-modified chips were characterized in the whole protein system (milk and soy whole protein) and single protein system (β-Lg) by SPR. As shown in Fig. 1 A and Fig. S2, ΔRU (the difference between the final response and the baseline response) of EK peptide-modified chips was significantly lower than that of the bare chip, and ΔRU of EK10 modified chips was the lowest. These results indicated that EK peptides exhibited significant resistance to non-specific protein adsorption regardless in a single protein system or whole protein system. In addition, EK10 presented a better antifouling ability than EK2 and EK5. AFM was used to characterize the antifouling property of the EK peptides. The protein adsorption on the surface was reflected by the changes in surface roughness and height after interaction with proteins (Fig. S1). The results of incubation with β-Lg, milk whole protein, and soy whole protein showed that the protein agglomeration on the EK10-modified chip was significantly reduced than that on the bare chip. Moreover, compared with these modified with EK2 and EK5, the protein agglomeration on the EK10 modified chip was the least, indicating that EK10 had better antifouling properties. The hydrophilicity of the EK peptide-modified surface was further studied by the WCA method. It was found that the WCA value of the bare chip reached 88.57°. In comparison, the WCA were 51.52°, 47.42°, and 42.91° for EK2, EK5, and EK10 modified surfaces, suggesting significantly improved hydrophilicity. The hydrophilicity arose from the EK peptide, which allowed the formation of a hydration layer through the electrostatic effect of the peptide. The hydration layer could inhibit proteins from getting close to the modified interface and thereby reduce the adsorption of the non-specific proteins (Ye et al., 2020). With excellent antifouling properties, EK peptides were further used in the following studies.

In recent years, magnetic particles have shown promising application potential in convenient separation and enrichment of targets in allergen detection methods (Y. Wang et al., 2023).Therefore, we employed the magnetic Fe3O4 particles as a basis for the capture probe in this work. To provide the magnetic nanoparticles with abundant binding sites for antifouling peptides, self-polymerization of dopamine on Fe3O4 was conducted to obtain Fe3O4@PDA particles. The SEM images showed that the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@PDA both exhibited a spherical structure and a reflective layer of PDA could be seen on the surface of Fe3O4@PDA (Fig. S3A). This indicated that Fe3O4 was superficially combined with PDA. TEM characterization (Fig. S3B) of Fe3O4@PDA also indicated the surface modification of PDA on Fe3O4. The hydrodynamic size of the Fe3O4@PDA was significantly larger than the Fe3O4 particles, suggesting the presence of the PDA layer (Fig. S3C). It was further proved by the difference in zeta potential (Fig. S3D) of the Fe3O4 particles (-54.7 ± 0.4 mV) and Fe3O4@PDA particles (-35.27 ± 0.53 mV). The FT-IR spectra of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@PDA particles (Fig. S3E) showed that the Fe3O4@PDA exhibited a corresponding CO stretching vibration peak at 1238 cm−1. A peak in the range of 1400 cm−1 to 1650 cm−1 corresponded to the skeleton vibration of the benzene ring, and 2927 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of CH compared with the Fe3O4 particles. This indicated that PDA was successfully coated on the Fe3O4 particle surface.

The antifouling abilities of EK peptides on Fe3O4@PDA particles to β-Lg, milk whole protein, and soy whole protein were further characterized. Fig. 1C shows the resistance to protein adsorption of β-Lg, milk whole protein, and soy whole protein. The results showed that the non-specific protein adsorption of the particles modified with EK peptide was significantly reduced compared with that of the control Fe3O4@PDA particles. Also, the protein adsorption on Fe3O4@PDA@EK10 was significantly reduced compared with that of Fe3O4@PDA@EK2 and Fe3O4@PDA@EK5. This indicated that EK10 possessed the best antifouling property among the three peptides, which was in accordance with the results from the EK peptide-modified planar surface. Therefore, the EK10 peptide was used to construct the capture probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Spatial arrangement of the anti-fouling and recognition ligands
ce:section.ce:para: The protein resistance of the EK peptide and the active recognition ability of APT1 are both crucial to the detection performance. As previous studies showed, the spatial arrangement of anti-fouling and recognition ligands played an important role in balancing the protein resistance and active recognition capabilities (Liu et al., 2021; G. Wang et al., 2018). Thus, the spatial arrangement of the EK10 peptide and APT1 was carefully studied. In detail, two modification strategies were adopted (Fig. 2 A): (1) simultaneously modifying EK10 and APT1 onto Fe3O4@PDA; (2) firstly modifying EK10 onto Fe3O4@PDA and then grafting APT1 on top of the EK10 layer. As can be seen from Fig. 2B, the surfaces constructed from the two strategies both showed similar antifouling ability to the milk and soy whole proteins. However, the binding amount of β-Lg on the particles from the second strategy was significantly higher than those of the control group and the particles prepared by the first strategy, indicating that the hierarchically modifying strategy could better balance the antifouling property and the active recognition functionality than the co-modifying strategy. Therefore, it was determined to use the hierarchically modifying strategy for the construction of the β-Lg capture probe in the subsequent experiments.

Next, the feeding ratio of the EK10 peptide and APT1 was optimized. Fig. 2C shows the amount of protein bound on the particles at different feeding ratios. For Group C (1.2 nmol EK10 and 100 pmol APT1), the protein adsorption amount of milk whole protein on the particle was significantly reduced, even comparable with the group that only modified with EK10. The protein adsorption amount of soy whole protein on the particles of Group C was also the least. Moreover, the ability to recognize β-Lg was similar among Group B to Group E. Considering the balance between nonspecific protein resistance and active recognition of β-Lg, the feeding ratio of Group C was chosen for the construction of β-Lg capture probe. The hydrodynamic size and zeta potential of the capture probe were further characterized (Fig. 3 B and Fig. 3C), which showed an increase in hydrodynamic size and more negative zeta potential than Fe3O4@PDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of the fluorescent recognition probe
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent recognition probe was constructed using Si-CD and amino group-terminated aptamer (APT2). From the UV–Vis spectra in Fig. 3D, it could be found that the fluorescent recognition probe showed a characteristic peak of APT at 260 nm in comparison to the Si-CD, indicating the successful modification of the APT2 on the Si-CD. The variation in the hydrodynamic size and zeta potential (Fig. 3E and Fig. 3F) also indicated the successful conjugation of the APT2. The fluorescent spectra (Fig. 3G) showed that Si-CD-APT2, like Si-CD, had a large emission peak at 520 nm when excited at 480 nm. Therefore, the Si-CD-APT2 could be applied as a recognition probe in the construction of the sandwich assay (Fig. 3A).
The detection conditions such as the type of buffer solution, the additive amounts of fluorescent recognition probes, and incubation temperature were optimized.

As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescent response during detection was relatively weak and the linear relationship was poor in water or PB. In contrast, β-Lg contents could be detected with higher sensitivity and better linear relationships in PBS. It could be due to that the ionic strength of PBS was mostly close to the binding buffer used in the aptamer (APT1) selection process (Eissa & Zourob, 2017), allowing better recognition of the target. Therefore, PBS was determined as the buffer solution for further experiments. Next, the additive amounts of the fluorescent recognition probes (50 pmol, 100 pmol, 200 pmol of APT2) were optimized. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence responses for the 50 pmol and 200 pmol groups were lower than the 100 pmol group. Also, the 100 pmol group showed a better linear relationship. For the 50 pmol group, the amount of recognition probe might be insufficient to give out an intense fluorescence signal. For the 200 pmol group, there could be fluorescence quenching due to the relatively high concentration of the recognition probe. Thus, the additive amount of the recognition probe was determined to be 100 pmol of APT2. Finally, the incubation temperature was optimized, and the results are shown in Fig. 4C. When the incubation temperature was 25 °C, the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of β-Lg concentration, and the fluorescent response was higher than these incubated at 30 °C or 37 °C. It may be due to the accelerated molecular movement at high incubation temperature, which increased the probability of fluorescence quenching. Therefore, 25 °C was determined as the optimized incubation temperature of the detection system.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: At the optimized conditions, the limit of detection (LOD), limit of quantitation (LOQ), specificity, and accuracy of the proposed method was evaluated.

The 0, 0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 4.0, and 5.0 µg/mL standard β-Lg solution was used to construct the standard curve (Fig. 5 A). The linear regression equation of the standard curve was y = 20701x + 6442 (R2 = 0.9903). The LOD (S/N = 3) and LOQ (S/N = 10) were calculated by a previously reported method (Gegenschatz et al., 2022), which were 0.035 μg/mL and 0.116 μg/mL, respectively. The method was more sensitive than an ultra-high liquid chromatography-based method (LOD, 7 µg/mL) (Boitz et al., 2015), and was comparable to the quartz crystal microbalance immune chip-based method (LOD, 0.04 μg/mL) (Pan et al., 2022).

To explore the specificity of the method, bovine serum albumin (BSA), ovalbumin (OVA), lactoferrin, glutenin, gliadin, soy protein isolate (SP), and α-casein (α-CN) were employed as nonspecific proteins. These proteins were selected beyond the following rules: 1) BSA was selected as it was the widely adopted model protein in the specificity test (Eissa & Zourob, 2017); 2) infant food formula mainly uses milk-based or soy-based protein sources. Therefore, lactoferrin, α-CN, and soy isolate protein were selected to investigate the potential interference from production line pollution; 3) OVA, glutenin, and gliadin were selected as representative nonspecific proteins from other allergen species that may be introduced through the production line. As shown in Fig. 5B, the proposed sandwich assay exhibited a relatively low cross-reactivity with non-specific proteins.

The accuracy of the method was verified by the recovery test. β-Lg standards were spiked in soy whole protein. The content of β-Lg was analyzed by the sandwich assay and the recoveries were calculated. As shown in Table S1, the recoveries were 98.75–119 %, falling in the criteria of 80–120 % (Burin et al., 2011). It suggested that the accuracy of the method was acceptable.

To verify the applicability of the sandwich assay in the commercial samples, β-Lg contents in infant formula, infant grain supplement, and infant canning supplement were detected and compared with the results measured by the commercial ELISA kit. As shown in Table 1 , the detection method showed comparable results with those of the ELISA kit. In addition, the samples with negative results were consistent with the labeling information on the packaging. The above results indicated that the sandwich assay possessed a high potential for the detection of β-Lg in commercial infant food. The β-Lg was chosen as the biomarker in this work since most people with milk allergies are sensitive to this protein (Y. Li et al., 2022). However, it was noticeable that β-Lg was labile to processing methods such as heat treating and autoclaving. On the contrary, caseins were heat-stable and could preserve the structural integrity upon harsh processing treatments (Villa et al., 2022). Therefore, there were pros and cons of using one or the other as molecular markers and it should be carefully chosen in the practical applications.

In this work, we successfully combined antifouling EK peptide and aptamer modification with the Fe3O4@PDA particles to construct a capture probe for β-Lg. The spatial arrangement of the EK peptide and aptamer was carefully tailored to obtain a capture probe with balanced antifouling ability and specific recognition properties. An aptamer-modified Si-CD fluorescent recognition probe was also developed and combined with the capture probe to construct a sandwich assay. The proposed sandwich assay exhibited the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 0.035 µg/mL (S/N = 3). The recoveries were 98.75–119 %, indicating acceptable accuracy of the method. In addition, the sandwich assay was used to analyze β-Lg in commercial infant food formula and the results were comparable to those determined by the commercial ELISA kit, suggesting the good practical application potential of the proposed method. The work paved a new way for tackling the dilemma between interference-resistance and active recognition and provided a strategy for the interference-free determination of food allergens in a protein-rich matrix.

Huan Li: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft, Funding acquisition. Donghui Jia: Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiangbing Shuai: Investigation. Xiaofeng Zhang: Methodology. Shunyu Wang: Resources. Min Wang: Writing – review & editing. Ke Li: Visualization. Linglin Fu: Supervision, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number LY21C200008 and LZ23C200001], and the Major Project of Digital Plus Discipline Construction of Zhejiang Gongshang University [grant number SZJ2022A010].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2023.137741.
